title: Chapter 11: Inspection
content: The car arrives at 10:47.

I know the time exactly because I've been standing at the window since nine, rehearsing. Not what I'll say — I know what I'll say. I've known since three in the morning, lying in my bed with Jessel's book open on my chest, committing her annotations to memory the way I used to commit case files. Underlining the important parts with my lips. Speaking them into the dark.

No. I've been rehearsing how I'll say it. The tone. The pacing. Professional, not hysterical. Factual, not emotional. The way I was trained to present findings to a committee that didn't want to hear them.

The black car rolls up the drive — not a taxi, not a rental. A car that belongs to someone, with a driver who opens the door from the outside. Henry Wingrave unfolds from the back seat and I think: of course. Of course this is what he looks like. Tall, silver-templed, wearing a coat that costs more than my quarterly salary. He moves like a man who has never once hurried in his life.

I step back from the window. Check the drawer where I've arranged everything. Jessel's copy of *The Turn of the Screw*, her annotations bleeding through the margins in three different pens — growing more frantic, less linear, as the pages progress. The tea sample, sealed in a clean jar, saved from the night I pretended to drink it. The photograph I took of the woman standing at the tree line in my coat — printed at the village shop, timestamp and all. The sleepwalking footage from my phone, four minutes of me walking the east wing corridor with my eyes closed, filmed from behind the door I'd propped open. And the ring. Miss Jessel's ring, found in the garden under the sundial, where Flora told me not to look.
Evidence.
I just need him to listen.

Mrs. Grose shows him in. She's polished the drawing room within an inch of its life — the silver, the glass, the wood. The whole house is performing, the way it always does for visitors. Even the light seems brighter, the windows cleaner, though I know for a fact nobody touched them.
"Maeve." Henry Wingrave extends his hand. His grip is dry, firm, precisely calibrated. "How are you settling in?"
"Mr. Wingrave. Thank you for coming."
"Henry, please." He smiles. Not unkindly. The smile of a man who wants you to feel comfortable because comfortable people say less. "Shall we sit?"

We sit. He takes the wingback by the fireplace — his, clearly, always has been, the leather worn in the shape of his body. He produces a flask from his coat pocket, pours two fingers into a crystal glass that materializes from the side table as if the house anticipated him. Gin. I catch it — the juniper, the faint sweetness — even from across the room.
Eleven in the morning and he's drinking, and the house has already prepared for it, and none of this strikes him as extraordinary.
"You wanted to speak with me," he says. "Mrs. Grose mentioned you had concerns."

"I do." My hands are steady. I place them flat on my knees, beneath the line of the table, where he can't see them. "I need to tell you what's been happening at Bly. And I need you to hear it before you decide what it means."
Henry sips his gin. "Of course."

I take out Jessel's book first. Slide it across the table. Open it to the first annotated page — the neat, methodical notes in blue pen. *Second night. Woke at 3 AM. Did not go to sleep. Found myself in the east wing with no memory of walking there.*

"This belonged to the previous governess. Miss Jessel. I found it in the schoolroom. She documented everything — her sleep disturbances, episodes of missing time, what she believed was deliberate gaslighting by the children."

I turn the pages. Let him see the progression — blue pen to black to red. The annotations climbing over each other, circling back, underlining phrases twice, three times. *They're doing this. They're doing this. They're doing this.*
Henry looks at the book. He doesn't touch it.

"I've been experiencing the same things," I say. "Sleep disruption. Lost time. I've found evidence of possible drugging — I saved a sample of the evening tea. The children have been coached to normalize my behavior when I'm experiencing these episodes, and to destabilize me when I'm not. I have video footage of myself sleepwalking. A photograph of an unknown woman on the property who was wearing my coat."
I place each item on the table. The jar. The photograph. My phone, cued to the footage. The ring, last, wrapped in a handkerchief.
"I believe Miss Jessel experienced something very similar before she left. And I believe the children are —"
The door opens.
"The children asked to speak with you," Henry says, already looking past me. "They're concerned."
I didn't hear him call for them. I didn't see him signal. But Mrs. Grose is already ushering them in, and then Miles and Flora are standing in the drawing room doorway, and the whole room changes.

They've dressed for this. I see it immediately — the clean starched collars, the brushed hair, Flora's small hands clasped in front of her like a Victorian portrait. Miles stands slightly ahead, shoulder angled toward his sister in a posture that reads as protective, brotherly. Concerned.
God, they're good.

"We're so worried about Maeve," Flora says. Her voice is small, precise, a crystal bell rung at the correct frequency. She steps forward and Henry's face softens — actually softens, the way faces do when children perform vulnerability in front of adults who want to believe them.
"She screams in the night," Miles says. Not dramatic. Quiet. The way you confess something that frightens you. "We can hear it from our rooms."
"We found her sleepwalking in the east wing," Flora continues, "and we were frightened."
I look at Miles. His face is open, earnest, the face of a boy who has practiced this expression in a mirror until it became second nature. Or until it became true — I don't know which is worse.
Flora's eyes fill with tears. She sniffles, once. "I just want her to get better."

The room holds this. The silence after a child cries. It's the loudest silence I've ever heard — louder than my evidence, louder than Jessel's annotated pages, louder than the gin hitting the crystal.
Henry sets down his glass. Nods slowly.

"Maeve." He turns to me and his expression has changed. It's still kind. That's the worst part. It's still kind, and that kindness is the blade. "The children told me about the episodes. Before you arrived." A pause. Weighted. "I should have been more transparent about the... challenges of the position."
Challenges.
The word lands in my chest like a stone in still water.

"The previous governess experienced similar difficulties," Henry continues. His voice is calm, practiced, a recitation delivered from a script he's read before. "Miss Jessel was a lovely young woman, but the isolation here — the distance from her support system — it became too much. I should have warned you. I thought—" He rubs his forehead. "I thought a fresh start might be different."

He reaches across the table. Picks up Jessel's book. Places it, gently, inside the leather folder he's brought with him — the one I assumed held employment records, contracts, the bureaucratic machinery of employing a governess. The book disappears inside. Then the photograph. Then the jar of tea. Then the ring, still wrapped in my handkerchief. He takes my phone, too — I let him, I don't know why I let him — and closes the folder.
"I've arranged for a doctor to visit," Henry says. "A psychiatrist. Someone who can help."

The word psychiatrist lands and I feel the floor shift beneath me, not because I'm surprised but because I'm not. Because this is what I should have expected. This is the move. This has always been the move.
My evidence is in his folder. My evidence is gone.

Not confiscated — absorbed. Neutralized. The way a body absorbs a splinter, pushing it deeper until it disappears under the skin. He didn't even look at it. Not really. He looked at me the way you look at someone who's showing you their art therapy project — with interest, with concern, with the gentle condescension of someone who knows this isn't really evidence, this is a symptom.
And I see it.

In his rehearsed calm. His careful distance. The way he never once touched Jessel's annotations with his fingertips, the way you'd handle something fragile and possibly contaminated. The way he said *challenges* instead of *problems* and *difficulties* instead of *abuse* and *episodes* instead of *crimes*. The way he knew the word *psychiatrist* before I opened my mouth.
He's done this before.

Not just with Jessel. Before Jessel. The one before. The one whose name I don't have yet, the one whose annotations I never found, the one who probably had her own jar of tea, her own photograph, her own ring. And before her, maybe another. A chain of women, each one arriving with their evidence, each one presenting it the same way I did — professionally, methodically, believing that facts matter.
He knows.

He's not protecting the twins from me. He's protecting the arrangement from disruption. The arrangement that keeps Bly Manor running, keeps the children in their beautiful prison, keeps the Wingrave reputation clean and the money flowing and the questions unasked. The children are part of it. Probably victims of it, too, once — trained so thoroughly they've become the trainers. But Henry isn't here to save them or to save me. He's here to maintain.
His kindness is the weapon. His reasonableness is the cage.
I understand this the way you understand a diagnosis — not with your feelings but with a cold, clear recognition that sinks through the blood and rearranges what you thought you knew.
Evidence doesn't matter here. Not because the evidence isn't real. Because the system that evaluates it is designed to produce only one conclusion.
"I understand," I say.
And I do. That's the terrible part. I understand completely.

Henry stands. The meeting is over. He's efficient about it — another practiced motion, the closing of a file, the folding of a problem. "The doctor will call on Thursday. In the meantime, try to rest. The children are very fond of you, Maeve. They want you to be well."
The children are very fond of you.
I hold perfectly still. I do not laugh. I do not scream. I say, "Thank you, Henry."

He pauses at the door. His hand on the frame, the gin still rising from his skin, and for one half-second I think I see something else in his face — a flicker, a crack in the glass. Something that might be guilt or might just be indigestion. Then it's gone, and he's stepping into the hall, and Mrs. Grose is receiving his instructions in a low voice I'm not meant to hear.
Miles and Flora remain. They don't speak while Henry's footsteps fade down the corridor. They're very still, the way children are when they're waiting to see if they've won.

Then Flora peels off from her brother and crosses to me in three quick steps and wraps her arms around my waist. She presses her face into my hip. She smells of lavender — the small-batch shampoo from the bathroom, the expensive kind, the kind that costs more than a governess earns in a week. Her hands are warm through my skirt.
"I'm so glad you're going to get help, Maeve," she murmurs.
I feel the performance in her body. The calculated pressure of the hug. The tremor in her voice that's just a little too precise.

But there's something else. Under the lavender and the warmth and the performance, there's a pulse. Her heartbeat, fast, faster than it should be for a child who just won a round. And her fingers, pressed into the fabric of my skirt — the right hand is hugging, but the left is curled, the tips pressing hard into the crease of my hip, and it doesn't feel like a hug. It feels like a grip on the edge of something.
I don't move. I let her hold me. I breathe in her lavender and I think: you're scared. You're scared and you're winning and those two things shouldn't coexist but they do.

Flora releases me and trots after Henry, and I hear Mrs. Grose calling her name, and for one moment — just one — Flora looks back at me from the doorway. Her eyes are dry. The tears are gone, evaporated as quickly as they came.
There's something in them that isn't triumph.
Almost — almost — apology.
Then she's gone.

I stand in the foyer after the car pulls away. I watch from the open door as Henry Wingrave folds himself back into the black car, the leather folder on the seat beside him. My evidence, heading down the drive, away, into the world that will never see it. The jar of tea will be dumped. The photograph will be lost. Jessel's annotations will be filed somewhere — or burned, more likely, though Henry wouldn't do something so dramatic. Henry would simply misplace them. Henry would forget they existed. Henry would look mildly surprised if anyone asked, and say, *What annotations?*

The twins stand on the front steps. They wave goodbye to their uncle with both hands, their small bodies swaying with the motion, because that's what children do on steps. They wave and they sway and they are so beautifully, devastatingly normal that I almost believe it myself.
Henry's car disappears through the gate. The iron bars close.
Miles turns.
He looks at me directly, in a way he never does when adults are watching. His face is open, unguarded, the mask dropped because the audience is gone and only the enemy remains. His lips curve.
It's a small smile. A private one. The smile of a boy who has studied a board game long enough to see fourteen moves ahead, and just watched his opponent walk exactly into the trap.
Checkmate.

I look back at him. The foyer is cold, the door still open, the wind should be biting but I don't feel it. My hands are at my sides. Empty — no evidence, no phone, no jar, no ring. Everything I gathered, everything I prepared, everything I believed would matter — gone into a leather folder, gone down the drive, gone.
Miles's smile holds.
But my face says something else.
Not surrender. Not defeat. Not the blank shock of a piece knocked off the board.
Recalculation.

I am thinking. I am thinking the way I thought in the weeks after my mother's death, when the doctors told me what I was feeling and I realized no one was going to ask me what I actually felt. I am thinking the way I thought in grad school, when my advisor handed back my first paper and said, *This is competent, Maeve, but competence is the enemy of brilliance* — and I rewrote it five times until it was brilliant. I am thinking the way I think when everything I've prepared is useless and I have to start over from a different angle, a different assumption, a different set of rules.
I believed evidence mattered. I was wrong.
I believed the right presentation would produce the right outcome. I was wrong.
I believed I was the investigator and they were the subjects. I was wrong.
So. New rules. New board. New game.

The door closes behind me. The foyer settles into its familiar silence. Somewhere in the house, Flora is humming — a melody I don't recognize, bright and careless, the sound a child makes when she's alone and doesn't have to perform.
Miles's smile falters. Just slightly. Just at the edges, where the skin around the eyes doesn't quite match the curve of the mouth.
He's trying to read my face.
He can't.
I climb the stairs. I don't look back. My footsteps are slow, deliberate, the pace of a woman who has nowhere to be and all the time in the world to get there.
Thursday. The psychiatrist comes Thursday.
I have until Thursday.

And then I think: no. That's wrong, too. That's still playing on their board. Thursday isn't the deadline. The deadline is the one they don't know about yet. The angle they haven't anticipated because they don't know I'm already thinking differently.
Flora's grip on my hip. Her too-fast heartbeat. The apology in her eyes that she didn't mean to show.

There's a crack in their board. A seam. A place where the performance doesn't quite meet the truth, where the mask slips and the breath comes faster than it should. Flora isn't as good at this as Miles is. Or maybe — maybe Flora doesn't want to be.

I reach the top of the stairs. Turn left, toward my room. The corridor is long and the light is grey and somewhere behind me Miles is still standing on the steps, trying to figure out what he's looking at.
Let him look.
I close my bedroom door. Sit on the edge of the bed. The empty drawer is open where I kept my evidence — Jessel's book, the jar, the photograph, the ring, the phone. All gone.

But not the memory. Not the annotations I committed to my lips in the dark. Not the taste of the tea I didn't swallow. Not the timestamp on the photograph, the angle of the woman at the tree line, the way she stood in my coat like she owned it. Not the footage I backed up to the cloud before I ever put my phone on that table.
Henry has the folder. He doesn't have my head.
I lie back on the bed and stare at the ceiling and I begin — slowly, carefully, with the patience of a woman who has lost everything she brought and nothing she actually needs — to recalculate.
The game isn't over.
It's just changed shape.
And I am very, very good at learning new rules.
